6 min read
1 hour ago

--

Your AI assistant can now query ArchPatterns directly. Plus trait profiles, side-by-side comparison, decision diffs, and full ADR generation, all provably stable, all still free.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

When I launched ArchPatterns, the promise was simple: make the architecture patterns canon retrievable at the moment of decision, through a deterministic selector that shows its working. The library grew quickly, to 323 curated entries across 22 domains, every one attributed to its canonical source.
Version 7.0 changes where that moment of decision can happen. Until now, ArchPatterns lived in your browser. From today, it also lives inside the tools where architecture decisions increasingly get made: your AI assistant, your coding agent, your scripts, your pipelines.
An MCP server: governed answers inside the AI workflow
Architecture advice from AI assistants has a known failure mode: fluent, plausible, and different every time you ask. The knowledge is in there somewhere, but it arrives unattributed, unversioned, and shaped by however the question was phrased.
ArchPatterns now runs a public Model Context Protocol (MCP) server at mcp.archpatterns.io. Connect it to any MCP-capable assistant, Claude, Cursor and a growing list of others, and the assistant gains six tools: run the selector, look up any of the 323 entries, search the library, compare candidates trait by trait, and check the exact library version. No sign-up, no API key.
The difference this makes is the difference between an assistant that improvises and an assistant that consults. Every response the server returns carries three things the improvised answer never has: the library version that produced it, a permanent link that reproduces it, and the attribution to the canonical literature it rests on. Ask your assistant to recommend an architecture for a single-team transactional system, and behind the fluent prose there is now a deterministic, citable engine: same answers in, same recommendation out, every time, for everyone.
I think of it as the difference between asking a colleague what they remember about a standard and having them open the standard. The conversation stays natural. The answer gains a spine.
A public JSON API, with a deliberate boundary
The same engine and library are available as a free JSON API. Every entry has a stable endpoint with its attributed definition, guidance and trait profile. The selector itself is an endpoint: the same encoded link that shares a recommendation between colleagues now returns that recommendation as JSON, cached at the edge, reproducible forever. Documentation lives at archpatterns.io/api.
One design decision is worth stating openly: there is no bulk download endpoint, by intent. Individual retrieval and citation are welcome, that is the entire point. The collection as a whole remains a curated database, protected as one, because the curation is the product. Take one brick freely; the wall stays where it is.
Trait profiles: the trade-offs, made visible
Inside the selector, every archetype and structural pattern now carries a trait profile: seven characteristics rated one to five, in the style Mark Richards and Neal Ford formalised in Fundamentals of Software Architecture. Scalability, deployability, fault tolerance, performance, simplicity, cost efficiency, evolvability.
Open Microservices and you see the honest shape of it: exceptional scalability and deployability, bought at the price of simplicity and cost. Open Layered Architecture and you see the mirror image. No pattern is good; every pattern is a trade. The profiles make that trade legible at a glance, and they are deliberately labelled as indicative, because a rating you cannot interrogate is just another opinion.
One honest boundary: practice-oriented entries, such as governance and enterprise architecture methods, carry no profile. Runtime characteristics do not apply to a review process, and pretending otherwise would be exactly the false precision the tool exists to avoid.
Compare: the shortlist, side by side
The new Compare view puts two or three candidates next to each other: definition, when to use, and full trait profiles aligned row by row. It is the conversation every design review actually has, “why this and not that”, given a permanent, shareable URL you can drop into a design document. The whiteboard argument becomes a citable artefact. And because it is also an MCP tool, your assistant can build the comparison for you.
Decision comparison: when two architects disagree
ArchPatterns recommendations have always been shareable links that reproduce the exact result. Those links now talk to each other. Paste a colleague’s shared recommendation next to yours and the tool shows precisely which answers diverge, question by question, and which recommendations each profile produces that the other does not.
This remains my favourite feature of the release, because it changes the nature of an architectural disagreement. “I think we need microservices” versus “I think we do not” is a debate about conclusions. The decision diff turns it into a debate about premises: we disagree because you answered enterprise-wide where I answered single team. Premises can be settled with evidence. Conclusions rarely can.
Full ADR generation
The selector now exports a complete Architecture Decision Record in MADR format: context and problem statement, your decision drivers taken from the wizard answers, considered options, the decision outcome with its fit score, consequences derived from the chosen option’s trait profile, and pros and cons of every option considered.
You still edit it, because an ADR must carry your solution’s specifics. But the skeleton, the alternatives, the trade-offs and the reproducible tooling citation are pre-populated, and the distance from decision to documented decision drops from an afternoon to minutes. Decisions that are easy to record are decisions that actually get recorded.
The release that tests itself
The least visible change matters most. The recommendation engine lives in an isolated, tested module, and every build runs dozens of locked scenarios against a frozen baseline, plus a contract test suite covering the API and the MCP server. If any change silently alters an established recommendation or breaks the public contract, the build refuses to ship. Changing a recommendation now requires a deliberate, recorded re-baselining.
For a product whose entire pitch is determinism, this is not plumbing. It is the promise, made checkable. “Cite ArchPatterns v7.0 in your ADR” only means something if v7.0 provably behaves like v7.0 forever, whether you reached it through the browser, the API, or your assistant. The full release history is published machine-readably at archpatterns.io/changelog.json, so reviewers and tools alike can see exactly what any cited version knew.
Unchanged, on principle
Everything that defines the product is exactly where it was. Deterministic and rule based: same answers, same recommendation, every time, on every surface. Every entry attributed to the canonical literature. No sign-up, no analytics; the on-site wizard still runs entirely in your browser. Free, in five languages, for every practitioner.
Try it on Thursday’s decision
ArchPatterns v7.0 is live at https://archpatterns.io, with developer documentation at archpatterns.io/api. Bring it the decision on this week’s calendar: run the wizard, compare your shortlist, share the link with the colleague who disagrees with you, and walk into the review with a MADR-format ADR instead of a hunch. Or connect mcp.archpatterns.io to your assistant and let it do the consulting, this time with sources.
Better decisions, made faster, and defensible on paper. That was the promise. Version 7.0 makes it available wherever the decision happens.

Wes Khan is an AI enablement and adoption leader, a doctoral candidate in AI and machine learning, and the author of the series “Closing the Enablement Gap”. ArchPatterns is a free, non-commercial personal project: 323 entries, 22 domains, one deterministic selector, at archpatterns.io.
